questions.question: Explain the difference between Throwaway Prototyping and Evolutionary Prototyping.
keyPoints: Throwaway prototyping creates a quick mockup to understand requirements and is discarded after use
Evolutionary prototyping builds on the prototype continuously to develop the final system
Throwaway saves time on documentation; evolutionary saves time on redevelopment

questions.question: List and briefly explain the four phases of the Spiral Model.
keyPoints: Planning: Define objectives, identify risks, and plan the iteration
Risk Analysis: Identify and analyze risks, develop mitigation strategies
Engineering: Develop and test the product
Evaluation: Customer evaluates the deliverables and provides feedback for next iteration

questions.question: What are the advantages of evolutionary process models over traditional sequential models?
keyPoints: Handles changing requirements effectively
Provides early and continuous customer feedback
Identifies defects and risks early in the development cycle
Allows for better risk management
Increases customer satisfaction through incremental delivery

questions.question: Explain the concept of prototyping in software engineering and its purposes.
keyPoints: Prototype is a preliminary version of software built quickly
Purposes: Understand requirements better, validate design decisions, get stakeholder feedback, reduce communication gaps
Types: Throwaway and Evolutionary prototyping

questions.question: What are the main disadvantages or risks associated with evolutionary process models?
keyPoints: Scope creep due to continuously changing requirements
Difficulty in predicting final cost and delivery schedule
May produce poorly structured code due to frequent changes
Managing customer expectations is challenging
Documentation may be inadequate as focus is on working code

questions.question: Explain the Iterative Enhancement Model with its working methodology.
keyPoints: Software is developed through repeated cycles (iterations)
Each iteration adds functionality to the existing system
Based on feedback from previous iteration
Starts with basic implementation and progressively enhances
Continues until the desired system is obtained

questions.question: Describe the Spiral Model of software development in detail. Explain how it handles risk management and why it is considered a combination of both iterative and waterfall models.
keyPoints: Spiral Model has four phases: Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, Evaluation
It is iterative - the spiral repeats until completion
Each iteration has risk analysis phase unique among models
Combines top-down approach (overall project planning) with bottom-up (prototype development)
Risk management through systematic identification, analysis, and mitigation of risks at each iteration
Cyclic approach allows for incremental releases of software
Advantages: flexible, handles changing requirements, early risk identification
Disadvantages: complex, requires expertise in risk analysis, may be expensive for small projects

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Waterfall Model with an evolutionary process model (choose any). Highlight scenarios where each would be appropriate.
keyPoints: Waterfall is sequential; evolutionary is iterative
Waterfall requires stable requirements; evolutionary handles changing requirements
Waterfall has phases completed before next; evolutionary overlaps and repeats
When to use Waterfall: Requirements well-understood, stable, technology fixed, short projects
When to use Evolutionary: Requirements unclear, complex projects, high-risk projects, need early customer feedback
Evolutionary provides better risk management but can lead to scope creep
Waterfall provides better documentation and structure but less flexible to changes

questions.question: Explain the Prototyping Model in software engineering. Discuss its types, advantages, and the challenges faced during prototype development.
keyPoints: Prototyping is an evolutionary model that quickly builds a preliminary version
Types: Throwaway (rapid) prototyping - mockup discarded; Evolutionary prototyping - prototype becomes basis for final system
Process: Requirement gathering → Quick design → Prototype building → Customer evaluation → Refinement → Final development
Advantages: Early user involvement, better requirement understanding, reduces risk of failure, early detection of design flaws
Challenges: Customer expectation management, prototype may be mistaken for final product, scope creep, inadequate documentation, may lead to shortcuts in quality

questions.question: Explain the iterative enhancement model of software development with a diagram. How does it differ from the pure waterfall approach?
keyPoints: Iterative Enhancement: Start with basic implementation, then iteratively refine adding more functionality
Each iteration: Plan → Design → Develop → Test → Evaluate → Feedback
Based on feedback, plan next iteration to enhance functionality
Difference from Waterfall: Not sequential; allows going back to earlier phases
Multiple cycles of development rather than single pass
Requirements can evolve through iterations
Early working version available for testing
Risk distributed across iterations rather than all at once
Better changing requirements
